title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lord Kettlemere and his daughter, Bondel, discuss the death of Gerry Wade, which occurred in Bondel's room. While Detective Largen suspects a political motive or an irregular cause, the death is officially treated as an accident or suicide. Tredwell reports that investigators found only Wade's fingerprints at the scene. During their conversation, Lord Kettlemere mentions that Wade has a much younger half-sister.

Later, Bondel attempts to assert her authority over the gardener, McDonald, by demanding he clear the overgrown tennis court and pick grapes out of season, though his response is ambiguous. Lord Kettlemere also reveals details regarding an "alarm clock incident," explaining that vacationers had hidden numerous alarm clocks in Wade's room as a prank. Following the death, the clocks were found arranged on a mantelpiece, though the servants deny touching them.

That night, Bondel reflects on the strangeness of the clocks. She recalls her maid mentioning that while seven clocks were on the mantelpiece, an eighth clock was found in the grass outside the window. Bondel considers whether Wade might have thrown the clock before dying, but concludes it was unlikely given his unconscious state. Seeking clarity, she decides to contact Bill Everleigh.

While preparing to write to Bill, Bondel discovers a wrinkled, unfinished letter in her desk drawer dated September 21st, the night before Wade was found dead. Addressed to Lorena, the note states that Wade is feeling well but tells her to forget about "the seven clock thing," which he had previously thought was a joke. The letter ends abruptly as Wade begins to mention someone named Rachel. Bondel notes two specific phrases in the text: "I'm feeling well" and "I'm so tired, my eyes won't stay open."
